prompt_a_response: A bishop moves diagonally in any direction.
A knight moves in an L-shape (two squares in one direction, then one square to the side).
A rook moves horizontally or vertically, any number of squares.
The queen moves any number of squares along a rank, file, or diagonal, any direction.
A pawn captures an enemy piece diagonally one square.
The pawn must be exchanged for a piece.

A player may legally respond to a check in one of the following three ways: 

1. By moving the king to a square where it is not under attack.
2. By capturing the checking piece with one of their pieces.
3. By interposing one of their pieces between the king and the checking piece.
Checkmate is the situation when a player's king is in a position to be captured (in check), and there is no way to move the king out of capture (no legal move to escape the check).
Stalemate is a situation where the player whose turn it is to move has no legal move, and their king is not in check. The game ends in a draw.
The two players can agree to a draw. It ends the game at the moment the agreement is made, during a game, provided that both players have made at least one move.

No, the game is drawn if the same position occurs three times, but only if the position occurs three times with the same player to move, or if a position occurs and the player to move can checkmate the opponent's king with a series of legal moves, and the same position occurs again with the same player to move, and the player to move can still checkmate the opponent's king with a series of legal moves, or 50 moves have passed without a capture or a pawn move, or the position is such that a checkmate is impossible. However, according to the FIDE Laws of Chess, the game is drawn if the same position occurs three times with the same player to move, and the claim is made by the player on the move.
Each side has 16 pieces and pawns: 1 king, 1 queen, 2 rooks, 2 knights, 2 bishops, and 8 pawns.
The player tips their king over.
The opponent's piece is captured and removed from the board.
On the square e1 for White's king and e8 for Black's king.

A pawn may capture an enemy pawn en passant under the following exact conditions: 

1. The enemy pawn moves two squares forward from its initial position.
2. The enemy pawn lands adjacent to the pawn that is making the en passant capture.
3. The capture is made on the next move, or the right to make the en passant capture is lost.
4. The pawn making the en passant capture moves diagonally to the square that the enemy pawn crossed.
Yes, the black pawn can capture en passant. It lands on a3.

No, a check that has since been dealt with does not permanently forfeit White's right to castle kingside. According to Article 3.8.2 of the FIDE Laws of Chess, castling is prevented only if the king has moved, or if the king or the rook involved has been under attack at any point, even if the check has been dealt with. However, in this scenario, since the king was checked but not moved, and the rook on h1 has not moved, White can still castle kingside.
They are obligated to move the touched piece, if it can be moved.
They must capture that piece with any piece that can do so.
They must first announce "j'adoube" or "I adjust".
The player is not obliged to move the touched piece.

A move is considered completed when the player has made a move, and has pressed the clock, or in the case of a digital clock, when the player has confirmed the move and the clock has started for the opponent.

In a standard-time-control game, players are required to record their moves. They must record their moves in algebraic notation, using a scoresheet, as the game progresses, until the player's time runs out or the game ends.
The player, however the organiser is entitled to keep the scoresheets for a reasonable period of time.

The scoresheet matters because the player must demonstrate that the same position has occurred at least three times or that 50 moves have passed without a capture or pawn move, and the scoresheet is the primary means of verifying this.
The player must write their intended move on the scoresheet, make the claim, and then wait for the arbiter's decision before making the move.
The same player must be on move, and the same castling rights and en passant capture possibilities must exist.
The claimant shall lose the right to make a draw claim on that move, and the game continues.
The player must claim the draw before making the 50th move, by stopping the clocks and informing the arbiter.
The 50-move rule is not an automatic draw, it must be claimed.
No. The game shall continue from the position immediately before the error occurred, with the original queen replaced and the extra queen removed.
The game shall continue from the starting position, and the clock shall be reset to its initial setting, but the time used before the discovery of the error shall not be taken into account.

The arbiter corrects the position on the board to the normal position, and the game continues from the corrected position, with the clock times adjusted to reflect the time taken to correct the error.
The game continues with the original position restored, and the players resume with the correct colors.
The player's move is considered complete, and the pawn is automatically promoted to a queen.
No. According to Article 3.1.1 of the FIDE Laws of Chess, intentional removal of a player's own king from the board is considered resignation, but accidental removal is not.
The player's original move, if it can be determined.

The result is a win for the delivering player, as this is a position of checkmate is not required for a win if the opponent has no legal move, this is known as a "stalemate win" or more formally as a win due to the opponent having no legal move, but the correct term for this is not stalemate, it's actually a win, however the FIDE laws call this situation a "checkmate" in article 6.2a and 6.2b and more specifically 6.9, but more accurately it is a win due to the position being such that the opponent has no legal move and is not in check but is unable to move, this situation is also sometimes referred to as "forced stalemate" in some chess literature but according to FIDE it is a win.
Loss for the player who checkmated, as their flag had fallen first.

The game shall continue from the position at the time the arbiter is called, and the illegal move is ignored. (FIDE Laws of Chess, Article 7.5b and 7.5c, and Article 7.6, and Article 7.7, and specifically 7.5b which states: "If a player has made an illegal move, their opponent can claim a win, unless the position arises from an illegal move of their own in the same game. The opponent can claim a win, provided they do this before making a move. If the opponent does not claim and the arbiter does not intervene, the illegal move stands and the game continues.")
